---
title: "Hands-on session: Data Analysis - Microbial communities (SCELSE Summer Course 2026)"
author: "Dr. Ezequiel Santillan"
date: "2026-June-26"
output:
  html_document:
    toc: true
    toc_float: true
    number_sections: true
    theme: cerulean
    highlight: tango
fontsize: 11pt
editor_options: 
  markdown: 
    wrap: 72
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
# Optional for interactive use.
# Mainly controls appearance of knitted HTML output.
# No need to run during class unless creating the HTML report
knitr::opts_chunk$set(
  echo = TRUE,
  warning = FALSE,
  message = FALSE,
  fig.align = "center"
)
```

[Script tested in R-version 4.4.1 // 2026-June-26]

**R Markdown**

This is an R Markdown document. Markdown is a simple formatting syntax
for authoring HTML, PDF, and MS Word documents. For more details on
using R Markdown see <http://rmarkdown.rstudio.com>.

When you click the **Knit** button a document will be generated that
includes both content as well as the output of any embedded R code
chunks within the document. 

**ACKNOWLEDGEMENTS** Dr Ezequiel (Ziggy) Marzinelli, original designer
of the Corallina sp. activity, including data and most of the
beta-diversity analysis code below. Such code below was expanded from
the SCELSE Summer Course 2019's Data Analysis Hands-on session, which I
helped Ziggy to conduct. Thanks also to Dr Soheil A. Neshat for bits
of code to help the script run in later R-versions and valuable
assistance during the teaching session.

**Remove every object, start with a clean session**

```{r chunk_clean_session}
rm(list=ls())

```

**Working directory**

No working directory setup is required for this practical, as all datasets are loaded directly from the public course GitHub repository.

For users wishing to work with local copies of the data, the working directory can be set manually through:

`Session` → `Set Working Directory` → `Choose Directory`

or:

```{r chunk_set_directory, eval=FALSE}
setwd("path/to/your/course/folder")
```

**Install and Load Packages** The traditional ways to install and load a
package is: *install.packages('ggplot2')* To install a package, we only
need to do this once.

To load a package, we use the following: *library(ggplot2)*

Alternatively, the function below ('packs') checks if the required
packages to run the code below are installed. [Code chunk courtesy of Soheil]
If the package is installed it will load the package, else it will
install the package and their dependencies (other packages that are also
required) and load the package.

```{r chunk_packages}
packs<-function(){
  for (package in c('vegan','ggplot2','ggpubr','rstatix','mvabund','car','pillar',
                    'tidyverse','multcompView','xfun','dplyr','tidyr','DiagrammeR'))
    {
    if (!require(package,character.only = T, quietly = T)){
      install.packages(package, dependencies = TRUE)
      library(package,character.only = T)
    }else{
      library(package,character.only = T)
      print(package)
    }
  }
}
packs()
```

# CASE 1: Bacterial communities in wastewater treatment bioreactors

*SEE EXPLANATORY SLIDES*

## OBSERVATIONS, MODEL and HYPOTHESIS

**Observations** Previous experiments with activated sludge bioreactors
subjected to 3-chloroaniline disturbance showed that intermediate
frequencies of disturbance demonstrated higher α-diversity and relative
influence of stochastic assembly compared to other exposure levels,
after a succession period of 35 days (**Santillan et al., npj Biofilms 
Microbiomes 5, 8 (2019)**, https://doi.org/10.1038/s41522-019-0079-4).

**Model** A possible explanation is that intermediate disturbance
frequencies gave rise to unpredictable environments for organisms
rendering their specialized traits less advantageous, stochastic
equalization of competitive advantages across the overall pool of
organisms would lead to a higher α-diversity. In contrast, either no
disturbance or press disturbance conditions at the extreme ends of a
disturbance range would allow fewer adapted organisms to dominate, thus
lowering the α-diversity. We named this causal relationship the
intermediate stochasticity hypothesis (ISH).

**Hypothesis** If the model is true, then it is predicted that
intermediate disturbance frequencies would promote stochastic assembly
processes resulting in increased α-diversity.

**Null Hypothesis** Intermediate disturbance frequencies would not
promote stochastic assembly processes resulting in increased
α-diversity.

**Test: EXPERIMENTAL DESIGN** We used an experimental system comprised
of activated sludge sequencing batch reactors harboring complex
microbial communities collected from a full-scale wastewater treatment
plant. These were subjected to different frequencies of alteration in
the feeding scheme of the substrate by doubling the organic carbon
content in the feed and keeping the nitrogen content constant. Such
alteration represents a disturbance for activated sludge systems due to
changes in competition for oxygen, substrate, and biofilm space. Indeed,
organic loading shocks were shown to affect relevant functions in
activated sludge systems, like carbon removal, sludge settleability, and
nitrification, as well as the overall structure and assembly of the
microbial community. In this study, the reactors had a working volume of
25 mL, representing a microcosm scale. Replicates (n = 5) received
double organic loading either never (L0, undisturbed), every 8, 6, 4, or
2 days (L1-4, intermediately disturbed), or every day (L5, press
disturbed), for 42 days (**Santillan and Wuertz, npj Biofilms Microbiomes 
8, 41 (2022)**, https://doi.org/10.1038/s41522-022-00301-3).

This design has one factor: 'Disturbance Frequency', fixed, categorical,
with 6 levels: 0, 1, 2, 3, 4 and 5. Each level has 5 independent
biological replicates (n = 5).

Download a data file from Santillan & Wuertz 2022:
<https://www.nature.com/articles/s41522-022-00301-3>
<https://static-content.springer.com/esm/art%3A10.1038%2Fs41522-022-00301-3/MediaObjects/41522_2022_301_MOESM2_ESM.txt>

```{r chunk_import_online_data}
## Import the data matrix and the factors data, and give them a name
dist_dat  <- read.table("https://static-content.springer.com/esm/art%3A10.1038%2Fs41522-022-00301-3/MediaObjects/41522_2022_301_MOESM2_ESM.txt", header = T) # Experimental data

dist_dat$L <- as.factor(dist_dat$L) # Set L as a categorical variable

# Lets see what are the variables in these data 
str(dist_dat)  # display the internal structure of the object 'dist_dat'

# Lets see if data were imported correctly
view(dist_dat)

## Change order of factor levels for plots so inoculum level "I" comes at the beginning:
dist_dat$L <- factor(dist_dat$L, levels = c("I", "0", "1", "2", "3", "4", "5"))

```

## Q: Does α-diversity change with time?

Let's first VISUALISE the data

### Plot α-diversity (Hill number #2)

```{r chunk_Hill_2_plot_1}
H2_d <- ggplot(data = dist_dat, mapping = aes(x = d, y = H2))+
               geom_point(size = 1, alpha = 1)
               
H2_d
```

```{r chunk_Hill_2_plot_2}
### We can modify aesthetics and color by disturbance level "L"
H2_d <- ggplot(data = dist_dat, mapping = aes(x = d, y = H2))+
               geom_point(aes(color = L), size = 2, alpha = 0.5)+
               theme_linedraw()+
               theme(
                      panel.grid.major = element_blank(), 
                      panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
                      axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 90),
                      text = element_text(size = 24)
                      )

H2_d
```

In this design, all 30 reactors across six disturbance levels started
from the same sludge inoculum. Four samples were randomly selected on
d0, these are assumed to represent initial conditions for all reactors
For temporal dynamics analysis, we can set the "I" level data as the d0
condition across all disturbance levels Data set is then processed to
remove level "I" and distribute inoculum sample values across all six
disturbance treatment levels for d0

TIP: We can ask ChatGPT (<https://chatgpt.com/>) for help coding this.
See example result below:

```{r chunk_day_0_data}

    # Step 1: Filter out rows where L == "I"
    rows_to_duplicate <- dist_dat[dist_dat$L == "I", ]

    # Step 2: Create a new data frame with the filtered rows
    original_data <- dist_dat[dist_dat$L != "I", ]

    # Step 3: Modify the L value in the new data frame to include all values except "I"
    new_rows <- do.call(rbind, lapply(unique(original_data$L), function(l_value) {
                       rows_to_duplicate$L <- l_value
                       rows_to_duplicate
                       }))

    # Step 4: Combine the modified rows back into the original data frame
    dist_dat_d0  <- rbind(original_data, new_rows)

# Let's see the structure of the modified data frame
str(dist_dat_d0)

# View the modified data frame
view(dist_dat_d0)

# Set L as a categorical variable
dist_dat_d0$L <- as.factor(dist_dat_d0$L) 
```

### Smooth lines of α-diversity (Hill number #2) vs day faceted by Level:

```{r chunk_Hill_2_plot_3}
H2_d <- ggplot(data = dist_dat_d0, mapping = aes(x = d, y = H2))+
               geom_point(size = 3, alpha = 0.5)+
               facet_grid(. ~ L)+
               geom_smooth(alpha = 0.5, se = TRUE, level = 0.95)+ # se = TRUE to show confidence intervals, e.g. "we are 95% confident that the parameter Y is between Y_low and Y_high"
               theme_linedraw()+
               theme(panel.grid.major = element_blank(), panel.grid.minor = element_blank())+
               theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 90))+
               theme(text = element_text(size=24))
               

H2_d
```
```{r chunk_COD_plot_3}
COD_d <- ggplot(data = dist_dat_d0, mapping = aes(x = d, y = COD))+
               geom_point(size = 3, alpha = 0.5)+
               #facet_grid(. ~ L)+
               #geom_smooth(alpha = 0.5, se = TRUE, level = 0.95)+ # se = TRUE to show confidence intervals, e.g. "we are 95% confident that the parameter Y is between Y_low and Y_high"
               theme_linedraw()+
               theme(panel.grid.major = element_blank(), panel.grid.minor = element_blank())+
               theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 90))+
               theme(text = element_text(size=24))
               

COD_d
```


Here 'loess' stands for "locally estimated scatterplot smoothing", see:
<https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Local_regression>

## Q: Is diversity different across groups?

Note that measurements taken at different time-points are not treated as
replicates (this would be pseudo-replication)

Rather, each time point considered here is a snap-shot where we can
evaluate our question.

Note about Time: A key aspect that differentiates time-series problems
from most other statistical problems is that in a time series,
observations are not mutually independent. Rather a single chance event
may affect all later data points. This makes time-series analysis quite
different from most other areas of statistics.

Such topic is beyond the scope of this course.

```{r chunk_data_d42}
### Filter data per day for statistical tests
dat_d42 <- filter(dist_dat, d == 42) #Data from d42 only        

### Get summary statistics (See: https://www.datanovia.com/en/lessons/compute-summary-statistics-in-r/)
sum_stats_d42 <- summarise(
                   group_by(
                     dat_d42, L
                   ),
                   n = n(),
                   mean = mean(H2),
                   sd = sd(H2),
                   min = min(H2),
                   max = max(H2)
                   ) 

# Print the resulting data frame
print(sum_stats_d42)
```

### Visualize the filtered data for Hill #2 in a box plot:

```{r chunk_Hill_2_d42}
boxP_H2 <- ggplot(data = dat_d42, mapping = aes(x = L, y = H2))+
                  geom_boxplot(notch = FALSE, fill = "lightgray", width = 0.3, outlier.size = 2)+
                  stat_summary(fun = mean, geom = "point", #This is to add the mean values to our plot
                               shape = 18, size = 4, color = "darkred", alpha = 0.6)+
                  geom_jitter(width = 0.1, alpha = 0.4)+ #This is to draw the data points too
                  theme_linedraw()+
                  theme(panel.grid.major = element_blank(), panel.grid.minor = element_blank())+
                  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 0))+
                  theme(text = element_text(size=24))

boxP_H2  # See plot
```

Elements of the box plots: - Tukey-style whiskers (most extreme data
point that is no more than 1.5 IQR from the edge of the box) - Red
diamond is the mean - Horizontal line is the median (Q2) - Lower box
edge is the lower quartile (Q1) - Upper box edge is the upper quartile
(Q3)

*SEE EXPLANATORY SLIDES*

## Q: Is diversity significantly different across groups?

### Welch's One-Way ANOVA Test:

Tests for equal means in a one-way design (not assuming equal variance).

This is is an alternative to the standard one-way ANOVA in the situation
where the homogeneity of variance assumption is violated, which is
usually the case for microbial systems.

```{r chunk_Hill_2_test}
H2_d42_test <- welch_anova_test(dat_d42, H2 ~ L)

   print(H2_d42_test)
```

We tested whether Hill diversity (H2) differed among treatments using Welch’s ANOVA. Like a standard one-way ANOVA, this test evaluates whether the variation among treatment means is larger than expected from the variation observed within treatments, but without assuming equal variances across groups, a condition that is often violated in microbial ecology datasets due to heterogeneous community responses and variability among treatments. The test detected a significant overall effect of treatment on H2 (Welch’s F = 23.88, df = 5, 10.92, P = 1.55 × 10⁻⁵), indicating strong evidence that not all treatment groups have the same mean Hill 2 diversity and that at least some groups differ from each other.

We can include the P-value value into the Hill 2 plot we made above.

```{r chunk_Hill_2_d42_anova_overlay}
# Extract p-value
p_val <- H2_d42_test$p

# Format p-value for display
p_text <- ifelse(p_val < 0.001, "P < 0.001", paste0("P = ", signif(p_val, 3)))

# Add the text to the plot
boxP_H2 +
  geom_text(data = data.frame(), 
            aes(x = -Inf, y = -Inf, label = p_text), 
            hjust = -0.1, vjust = -1.2, size = 6, inherit.aes = FALSE)
```

## Q: Which groups are significantly different from each other in terms of diversity?

### Games-Howell Post-Hoc testing:

Performs a Games-Howell test, which is used to compare all possible
combinations of group differences when the assumption of homogeneity of
variances is violated. This post hoc test provides confidence intervals
for the differences between group means and shows whether the
differences are statistically significant.

The test is based on Welch's degrees of freedom correction and uses
Tukey's studentized range distribution for computing the p-values. The
test compares the difference between each pair of means with appropriate
adjustment for the multiple testing. So there is no need to apply
additional p-value corrections.

```{r chunk_Hill_2_post_hoc}
H2_d42_PH_group  <- games_howell_test(dat_d42, H2 ~ L)

  print(H2_d42_PH_group)
```

**Interpreting the output of the Games-Howell test** - Levels 0, 1 and 4
are not significantly different [Group A] - Levels 1, 2, 3 and 4 are not
significantly different [Group B] - Level 5 are significantly different
from all other levels [Group C]

The package 'multcompView' can help us automate this analysis as follows.

```{r chunk_Hill_2_groups}
# Create vector of p-values with comparisons
pvals <- H2_d42_PH_group %>%
  mutate(comp = paste(group1, group2, sep = "-")) %>%
  select(comp, p.adj) %>%
  deframe()

# Generate group letters
group_letters <- multcompLetters(pvals, threshold = 0.05)

# Print group assignments
print(group_letters$Letters)

# Count number of distinct groups
n_groups <- length(unique(group_letters$Letters))
cat("There are", n_groups, "statistically distinct groups.\n")
```
Note that this package identifies 4 different groups instead of 3, considering "ab" as a different group.

We can include this information into the Hill 2 plot.

```{r chunk_Hill_2_d42_groups_overlay}
# Create vector of p-values with comparisons
pvals <- H2_d42_PH_group %>%
  mutate(comp = paste(group1, group2, sep = "-")) %>%
  select(comp, p.adj) %>%
  deframe()

# Generate group letters
group_letters <- multcompLetters(pvals, threshold = 0.05)

# Convert to a data frame for plotting
group_labels <- data.frame(
  L = factor(names(group_letters$Letters), levels = levels(dat_d42$L)),
  label = group_letters$Letters
)

# Overlay group letters onto the boxplot
boxP_H2 +
  geom_text(data = group_labels, aes(x = L, y = max(dat_d42$H2) + 3, label = label),
            size = 6, vjust = 0)
```

We can include both the Welch's ANOVA P-value and Games-Howell post-hoc grouping into the Hill 2 plot.

```{r chunk_Hill_2_d42_groups_pval_overlay}
library(multcompView)
library(dplyr)

# Generate group letters
pvals <- H2_d42_PH_group %>%
  mutate(comp = paste(group1, group2, sep = "-")) %>%
  select(comp, p.adj) %>%
  deframe()

group_letters <- multcompLetters(pvals, threshold = 0.05)

group_labels <- data.frame(
  L = factor(names(group_letters$Letters), levels = levels(dat_d42$L)),
  label = group_letters$Letters
)

# Format Welch's ANOVA p-value
p_val <- H2_d42_test$p
p_text <- ifelse(p_val < 0.001, "P < 0.001", paste0("P = ", signif(p_val, 3)))

# Combine both overlays
boxP_H2 +
  geom_text(data = group_labels, aes(x = L, y = max(dat_d42$H2) + 3, label = label),
            size = 6, vjust = 0) +
  geom_text(data = data.frame(), aes(x = -Inf, y = -Inf, label = p_text),
            hjust = -0.1, vjust = -1.2, size = 6, inherit.aes = FALSE)
```



*SEE EXPLANATORY SLIDES*




# CASE 2: Macroalga-associated bacterial communities

Ziggy's 2019 Hands-on example (w/ add-ons from Eze). For the hands-on
session on statistics, we will analyse microbial community data obtained
via sequencing. As explained during the lectures, these data are
'multivariate', i.e. it can be described as a matrix of n samples x p
variables (e.g. OTUs or ASVs).

Typically, those samples come from different groups (categories or
factors), or are taken from a continous environmental gradient (e.g.
soil samples across a pH gradient). In this example, samples are
host-associated bacterial communities, where the host is a species of
seaweed (macroalga), which was sampled from different places.

*SEE EXPLANATORY SLIDES*

## OBSERVATIONS, MODEL and HYPOTHESIS

**Observations** Previous experiments showed that the settlement of
sea-urchin larvae is influenced by a species of seaweed, Corallina sp.,
which occurs in similar abundances across Sydney Harbour, Australia.

Some bays within Sydney Harbour have storm-water drains, while others
don't. Storm-water can transport nasty contaminants from land to the
water, which can affect biodiversity. The presence and amounts of
contaminants such as agrochemicals, heavy metals, etc. have been shown
to be significantly greater in bays with storm-water drains than in bays
without drains.

Sampling of sea-urchin larvae in highly contaminated bays (+ drains) and
those with low contamination levels (no drains) showed that there is
much lower settlement in the high contamination places.

Seaweeds produce chemicals that can influence the structure of
surface-associated microbiomes. **Model 1** One possible model or
explanation for the differences in the observed larval settlement
patterns is that the microbial communities associated to Corallina sp.
in high vs low contaminated areas are different, and may therefore
produce different settlement cues, influencing differential settlement
of sea-urchin larvae.

**Hypothesis 1** If the model is true, then it is predicted that the
microbial community structure would differ between high vs low
contaminated areas.

**EXPERIMENTAL DESIGN**

Microbial communities on Corallina were sampled at 4 highly contaminated
bays and 4 bays with low levels of contamination. n=3 seaweed
individuals were sampled in each bay.

NOTE that there needs to be replication of bays for both high vs low
contamination because of the issue of replication at the right level
(see Experimental Design lectures).

Therefore, we have 2 factors (categorical covariates): -
'Contamination', fixed, with 2 levels: High vs Low - 'Site', random,
nested within Contamination, with 4 sites for each contamination level

EXERCISE: Draw an schematic of the experimental design.
We can also use the package DiagrammeR to do so, as follows:

```{r chunk_design_schematic}
grViz("
digraph experimental_design {

  # Node definitions
  node [shape=box, style=rounded]

  Contamination [label='Contamination']
  Low [label='Low']
  High [label='High']

  # Sites for Low Contamination
  a [label='Site a']
  b [label='Site b']
  c [label='Site c']
  d [label='Site d']

  # Sites for High Contamination
  e [label='Site e']
  f [label='Site f']
  g [label='Site g']
  h [label='Site h']

  # Edges
  Contamination -> Low
  Contamination -> High

  Low -> a
  Low -> b
  Low -> c
  Low -> d

  High -> e
  High -> f
  High -> g
  High -> h
}
")

```


**Microbial Data** We have a matrix of samples x variables, in this case
OTU (bacterial classes).

We can compare communities between the macroalgal hosts by doing
dimension reduction on the data through: (1) alpha diversity univariate
analyses (2) distance-based beta-diversity multivariate analyses

## Alpha diversity analyses to compare community structure

The datasets used in this practical are hosted in the public course GitHub repository and are imported directly from there. This avoids the need to download files or configure working directories, ensuring that the code runs identically across operating systems.

```{r chunk_data_import}
## Import the data matrix and the factors data directly from the course GitHub repository

Dat <- read.delim(
  "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/ezesantillan/SCELSE-summer-course-experimental-design-data-analysis/main/data/OTU.txt",
  row.names = 1
)        # OTU data

Fac1 <- read.delim(
  "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/ezesantillan/SCELSE-summer-course-experimental-design-data-analysis/main/data/Factors.txt",
  row.names = 1
)        # Factor metadata

# Let's see if the data were imported correctly
head(Dat)
head(Fac1)

# Let's inspect the variables in these datasets
str(Dat)   # display the internal structure of the object 'Dat'
str(Fac1)

# How many samples and variables does the data matrix contain?
dim(Dat)

# 45 variables (OTUs), 24 samples
# (2 contamination levels × 4 sites within each contamination level × 3 replicates = 24)
```

### Data Pre-processing

Below are the usual steps that people working on similar datasets apply.
However, see McMurdie & Holmes 2014, PLOS Comput. Biol.
<https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pcbi.1003531> and also see Weiss et al.
2017, Microbiome. <https://doi.org/10.1186/s40168-017-0237-y>.

```{r chunk_data_preprocessing}
tDat = t(Dat) # transpose the data as vegan wants variables (taxa, OTUs, ASVs, etc.) as columns

S <- specnumber(tDat) # observed number of species (Richness)
head(S)
range(S) # We observe that richness varies from 15 to 26 taxa, but we didn't account yet for differences in sequencing depth

#install.packages('vegan') #need to install this if you haven't
#library(vegan) #load the R package 'vegan

# Higher order alpha diversity indices  (Hill, 1973)
H0 <- S #Renaming richness to Hill #0
H1 <- exp(diversity(tDat, index = "shannon"))
H2 <- diversity(tDat, index = "invsimpson")

# Make a dataframe with Hill numbers (including metadata)
Hill <- data.frame(Fac1, H0, H1, H2)
head(Hill)

## Change order of factor levels for plots so that "Low" comes first:
Hill$Contamination <- factor(Hill$Contamination, levels = c("Low", "High"))
```

**Visualize the data** install.packages('ggplot2') #need to install this
if you haven't library(ggplot2) #load the R package 'ggplot2'

###Plot Hill numbers using box plots - Tukey-style whiskers (most
extreme data point that is no more than 1.5 IQR from the edge of the
box) - Red diamond is the mean - Horizontal line is the median (Q2) -
Lower box edge is the lower quartile (Q1) - Upper box edge is the upper
quartile (Q3)

#### Hill #0 (Richness) alpha diversity:

```{r chunk_e19e33}
box_H0 <- ggplot(data = Hill, mapping = aes(x = Contamination, y = H0))+
                 geom_boxplot(notch = FALSE, fill = "lightgray", width = 0.3, outlier.size = 2)+
                 stat_summary(fun = mean, geom = "point", #To add the mean values to our plot
                              shape = 18, size = 4, color = "darkred", alpha = 0.6)+
                 geom_jitter(width = 0.15, alpha = 0.4)+ #To draw the data points too      
                 theme_linedraw()+
                 theme(panel.grid.major = element_blank(), panel.grid.minor = element_blank())+
                 theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 0))+
                 theme(text = element_text(size=15))

box_H0  # See plot
```

#### Hill #1 (exp. Shannon index) alpha diversity

```{r chunk_d43b76}
box_H1 <- ggplot(data = Hill, mapping = aes(x = Contamination, y = H1))+
                 geom_boxplot(notch = FALSE, fill = "lightgray", width = 0.3, outlier.size = 2)+
                 stat_summary(fun = mean, geom = "point", #To add the mean values to our plot
                              shape = 18, size = 4, color = "darkred", alpha = 0.6)+
                 geom_jitter(width = 0.15, alpha = 0.4)+ #To draw the data points too      
                 theme_linedraw()+
                 theme(panel.grid.major = element_blank(), panel.grid.minor = element_blank())+
                 theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 0))+
                 theme(text = element_text(size=15))

box_H1  # See plot
```

#### Hill #2 (inverse Simpson) alpha diversity

```{r chunk_a4f300}
box_H2 <- ggplot(data = Hill, mapping = aes(x = Contamination, y = H2))+
                 geom_boxplot(notch = FALSE, fill = "lightgray", width = 0.3, outlier.size = 2)+
                 stat_summary(fun = mean, geom = "point", #To add the mean values to our plot
                              shape = 18, size = 4, color = "darkred", alpha = 0.6)+
                 geom_jitter(width = 0.15, alpha = 0.4)+ #To draw the data points too      
                 theme_linedraw()+
                 theme(panel.grid.major = element_blank(), panel.grid.minor = element_blank())+
                 theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 0))+
                 theme(text = element_text(size=15))

box_H2  # See plot
```

#### Hill numbers 0, 1, and 2

We can visualize these together by using the 'ggarrange' function from
the package 'ggpubr' install.packages('ggpubr') #need to install this if
you haven't library(ggpubr) #load the R package 'ggpubr'

```{r chunk_5e1a04}
ggarrange(box_H0, box_H1, box_H2, ncol = 3, nrow = 1) ### See plots
```

**Interpretation of alpha-diversity plots** We observe that there are
differences in higher order Hill number indexes, meaning that
communities may be different not in composition but in relative
abundances.

There also seems to be higher variability of H1 and H2 at Low
contamination sites than for High contamination ones

But: Where are the 12 points distributed along these box plots? Note
that there are also four different sites sampled per each level of
contamination.

### Raincloud Plots

A more informative (and fancier) alternative are Raincloud Plots. See:
<https://www.cedricscherer.com/2021/06/06/visualizing-distributions-with-raincloud-plots-with-ggplot2/#back1>

```{r chunk_822728}
#For R-versions above 3.6.3, you might need to set these below as factors
Fac1$Contamination<-as.factor(Fac1$Contamination)
Fac1$Site<-as.factor(Fac1$Site)
```

#### Hill #0 rain-cloud plot:

```{r chunk_208841}
rc_H0 <- ggplot(data = Hill, aes(x = Contamination, y = H0)) + 
         ## add half-violin from {ggdist} package
                ggdist::stat_halfeye(
         ## custom bandwidth
                adjust = .5, 
         ## adjust height
                width = .35, 
         ## move geom to the right
                justification = -.35, 
         ## remove slab interval
                .width = 0, 
                point_colour = NA
               ) + 
         ## add dot plots from {ggdist} package
            ggdist::stat_dots(
         ## orientation to the left
                           side = "left", 
         ## move geom to the left
                           justification = 1.15, 
         ## adjust grouping (binning) of observations 
                           binwidth = .15,
         ## adjust dot size
                           dotsize = 1.4,
         ## color by site
                           color = c("black","red","blue","orange","brown","green","yellow","cyan") [as.numeric(Fac1$Site)]
                            ) + 
         ## remove white space on the left
            coord_cartesian(xlim = c(1.2, NA))+
         #Now add previous code for box-plot, mean, aesthetics, etc.
         geom_boxplot(notch = FALSE, fill = "lightgray", width = 0.15, outlier.size = 1)+
                      stat_summary(fun = mean, geom = "point",
                      shape = 18, size = 3, color = "darkred", alpha = 0.5)+
                      theme_linedraw()+
                      theme(panel.grid.major = element_blank(), panel.grid.minor = element_blank())+
                      theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 0))+
                      theme(text = element_text(size=15))

rc_H0 #See plot
```

#### Hill #1 rain-cloud plot

```{r chunk_650be7}
rc_H1 <- ggplot(data = Hill, aes(x = Contamination, y = H1)) + 
  ## add half-violin from {ggdist} package
  ggdist::stat_halfeye(
    ## custom bandwidth
    adjust = .4, 
    ## adjust height
    width = .20, 
    ## move geom to the right
    justification = -.75, 
    ## remove slab interval
    .width = 0, 
    point_colour = NA
  ) + 
  ## add dot plots from {ggdist} package
  ggdist::stat_dots(
    ## orientation to the left
    side = "left", 
    ## move geom to the left
    justification = 1.15, 
    ## adjust grouping (binning) of observations 
    binwidth = .15,
    ## adjust dot size
    dotsize = 0.8,
    ## color by site
    color = c("black","red","blue","orange","brown","green","yellow","cyan") [as.numeric(Fac1$Site)]
  ) + 
  ## remove white space on the left
  coord_cartesian(xlim = c(1.2, NA))+
  #Now add previous code for box-plot, mean, aesthetics, etc.
  geom_boxplot(notch = FALSE, fill = "lightgray", width = 0.15, outlier.size = 1)+
  stat_summary(fun = mean, geom = "point",
               shape = 18, size = 3, color = "darkred", alpha = 0.5)+
  theme_linedraw()+
  theme(panel.grid.major = element_blank(), panel.grid.minor = element_blank())+
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 0))+
  theme(text = element_text(size=15))

rc_H1 #See plot
```

#### Hill #2 rain-cloud plot

```{r chunk_20a719}
rc_H2 <- ggplot(data = Hill, aes(x = Contamination, y = H2)) + 
  ## add half-violin from {ggdist} package
  ggdist::stat_halfeye(
    ## custom bandwidth
    adjust = .3, 
    ## adjust height
    width = .16, 
    ## move geom to the right
    justification = -.85, 
    ## remove slab interval
    .width = 0, 
    point_colour = NA
  ) + 
  ## add dot plots from {ggdist} package
  ggdist::stat_dots(
    ## orientation to the left
    side = "left", 
    ## move geom to the left
    justification = 1.15, 
    ## adjust grouping (binning) of observations 
    binwidth = .15,
    ## adjust dot size
    dotsize = 0.6,
    ## color by site
    color = c("black","red","blue","orange","brown","green","yellow","cyan") [as.numeric(Fac1$Site)]
  ) + 
  ## remove white space on the left
  coord_cartesian(xlim = c(1.2, NA))+
  #Now add previous code for box-plot, mean, aesthetics, etc.
  geom_boxplot(notch = FALSE, fill = "lightgray", width = 0.15, outlier.size = 1)+
  stat_summary(fun = mean, geom = "point",
               shape = 18, size = 3, color = "darkred", alpha = 0.5)+
  theme_linedraw()+
  theme(panel.grid.major = element_blank(), panel.grid.minor = element_blank())+
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 0))+
  theme(text = element_text(size=15))

rc_H2 #See plot
```

#### Arrangle rain-cloud plots for all three Hill indexes:

```{r chunk_103c25}
ggarrange(rc_H0, rc_H1, rc_H2, ncol = 3, nrow = 1) ### See plots
```

**Interpretation** Are the observed differences significant? Now that we
have visualized the data, we can run a few tests to see if the
differences we observe are statistically significant

### Hypothesis testing:

#### One-way ANOVA tests

Site nested in Contamination is random, i.e. that term gives information
on variability in communities between sites. Our main interest is in the
effect of contamination. Is this significant? What does this mean?

The code below indicates that we are fitting an ANOVA model where Site
is nested within Contamination. The '/' operator specifies this nesting,
meaning that Site is a subcategory within each Contamination level.

##### ANOVA on Hill #0

```{r chunk_8142e8}
nest_H0 <- aov(Hill$H0 ~ Hill$Contamination / factor(Hill$Site)) # the '/' means that Site is nested within Contamination
summary(nest_H0)
```

##### ANOVA on Hill #1

```{r chunk_49affb}
nest_H1 <- aov(Hill$H1 ~ Hill$Contamination / factor(Hill$Site)) # the '/' means that Site is nested within Contamination
summary(nest_H1)
```

##### ANOVA on Hill #2

```{r chunk_27e9a4}
nest_H2 <- aov(Hill$H2 ~ Hill$Contamination / factor(Hill$Site)) # the '/' means that Site is nested within Contamination
summary(nest_H2)
```

#### Interpreting the Components of the ANOVA Table:

##### Degrees of Freedom (Df):

- **Hill$Contamination**: 1 degree of freedom. This reflects the number of independent groups for the factor **Contamination** (e.g., High vs Low).
- **Hill$Site (nested within Contamination)**: 6 degrees of freedom. This represents the number of independent ways by which **Sites within each Contamination level** can vary.
- **Residuals**: 16 degrees of freedom. This reflects the leftover, unexplained variation in the response variable.

##### Sum of Squares (Sum Sq):

- **Hill$Contamination**: 28.515. This represents the variation in the response variable that can be attributed to differences between Contamination levels.
- **Hill$Site (nested within Contamination)**: 21.905. This represents the variation attributed to differences among Sites within each Contamination level.
- **Residuals**: 3.585. This is the unexplained variation (error) in the response variable.

##### Mean Squares (Mean Sq):

- **Hill$Contamination**: 28.515. Calculated as Sum Sq / Df for **Contamination**.
- **Hill$Site (nested within Contamination)**: 3.651. Calculated as Sum Sq / Df for **Site** within Contamination.
- **Residuals**: 0.224. Calculated as Sum Sq / Df for the residuals.

##### F value:

- **Hill$Contamination**: 127.3. The ratio of the Mean Sq for **Contamination** to the Mean Sq for **Site nested within Contamination** (28.515 / 3.651). In nested designs, this is the correct denominator to test the effect of Contamination.
- **Hill$Site (nested within Contamination)**: 16.3. The ratio of the Mean Sq for **Site** to the Mean Sq for the Residuals (3.651 / 0.224).

##### Pr(>F):

- **Hill$Contamination**: 5.01e-09. The p-value for the F-test of **Contamination**, accounting for the nested structure. A very small p-value indicates a highly significant effect of Contamination.
- **Hill$Site (nested within Contamination)**: 5.27e-06. The p-value for the F-test assessing variability among Sites within each Contamination level.

##### Significance Codes:

**\*\*\***: p-value ≤ 0.001, indicating very strong evidence against the null hypothesis.

##### Interpretation:

- There is a highly significant effect of **Contamination** on the response variable, as indicated by the large F value (127.3) and the very small p-value (5.01e-09).
- There are also significant differences among **Sites within Contamination levels**, supported by the F value (16.3) and p-value (5.27e-06).
- The **Residuals** represent remaining unexplained variation, which is relatively small compared to the explained variation.

##### Conclusion:

The nested ANOVA shows that both the **Contamination** factor and the variability among **Sites within each Contamination level** contribute significantly to overall variation in the response variable. The structure of the nested design was properly accounted for, and the results indicate clear differences between Contamination levels and among Sites within those levels.

### ANOVA assumptions

But wait, are the test's assumptions met? 
1) Normality 
2) Non-heteroscedasticity (constancy of variance) 
3) Observations are independent \<--- This depends on the experimental design!

#### Normality assumption:

**Shapiro-Wilk normality test** When p-values are greater than the
significance level 0.05, this implies that the distribution of the data
is not significantly different from the normal distribution. In other
words, we can assume normality.

##### Shapiro-Wilk normality test on Hill numbers

```{r chunk_fa8454}
shapiro.test(Hill$H0)

shapiro.test(Hill$H1)

shapiro.test(Hill$H2)
```

##### Q-Q plots:

An alternative is to visually inspect the data normality using Q-Q plots
(quantile-quantile plots). Q-Q plots draw the correlation between a
given sample and the normal distribution.

```{r chunk_12dc43}
ggqqplot(Hill$H0, ylab = "H0")

ggqqplot(Hill$H1, ylab = "H1")

ggqqplot(Hill$H2, ylab = "H2")
```

#### Constancy of variance

**Levene Test (robust to non-normality)** The null hypothesis is that
all populations variances are equal, the alternative hypothesis is that
at least two of them differ. Consequently, p-values less than 0.05
suggest variances are significantly different and the homogeneity of
variance assumption has been violated.

install.packages('rstatix') #need to install this if you haven't
library(rstatix) #load the R package 'rstatix'

##### Levene tests on Hill numbers:

```{r chunk_b040af}
levene_test(Hill$H0 ~ Hill$Contamination, data = Hill) 

levene_test(Hill$H1 ~ Hill$Contamination, data = Hill) 

levene_test(Hill$H2 ~ Hill$Contamination, data = Hill) 
```

Since the assumption of constancy of variance is not met, we need to use
a test that does not assume equal variance.

#### Welch one-way ANOVA test (not assuming equal variance, also robust against non-normality)

```{r chunk_Welch_ANOVA_Hill_Case_2}
Hill %>% welch_anova_test(H0 ~ Contamination / Site)  # the '/' means that Site is nested within Contamination

Hill %>% welch_anova_test(H1 ~ Contamination / Site)  # the '/' means that Site is nested within Contamination

Hill %>% welch_anova_test(H2 ~ Contamination / Site)  # the '/' means that Site is nested within Contamination
```


##### Explanation of the Output:

The table presents the results of Welch's ANOVA for each of the response variables H0, H1, and H2. This method is robust to unequal variances and accommodates the nested design structure where **Site** is nested within **Contamination**.

###### For H0:

- **.y.**: The response variable tested, H0.
- **n**: Number of observations, 24.
- **statistic**: The F-statistic from Welch's ANOVA is 3.45, measuring the ratio of between-group to within-group variance.
- **DFn**: Degrees of freedom for the numerator (between groups), 7.
- **DFd**: Degrees of freedom for the denominator (within groups), approximately 6.70, adjusted for unequal variances.
- **p**: 0.066. This p-value suggests that the differences among groups are not statistically significant at the 0.05 threshold.
- **method**: Welch ANOVA, which adjusts for heterogeneity of variances.

**Interpretation**: There is no statistically significant difference in H0 among the groups defined by Contamination and Site.

###### For H1:

- **.y.**: H1, the response variable.
- **n**: 24 observations.
- **statistic**: 62.82, indicating strong between-group differences relative to within-group variability.
- **DFn**: 7.
- **DFd**: Approximately 6.74.
- **p**: 1.24e-05, a very small p-value indicating strong evidence against the null hypothesis.
- **method**: Welch ANOVA.

**Interpretation**: There are significant differences in H1 across the groups defined by the nested factor Contamination and Site.

###### For H2:

- **.y.**: H2, the response variable.
- **n**: 24 observations.
- **statistic**: 126.62, indicating very large between-group variability.
- **DFn**: 7.
- **DFd**: Approximately 6.21.
- **p**: 3.01e-06, indicating extremely strong evidence against the null hypothesis.
- **method**: Welch ANOVA.

**Interpretation**: There are highly significant differences in H2 among the groups defined by Contamination and Site.

##### General Notes:

- The **DFn** (7) reflects the number of independent group comparisons being made, considering the nested design.
- The **DFd** is adjusted for heterogeneity of variances; non-integer values are expected with Welch's ANOVA.
- A small p-value (typically < 0.05) indicates statistically significant group differences.

##### Conclusion:

The Welch's ANOVA results show that:
- For **H0**, there is no significant effect of Contamination and Site.
- For **H1** and **H2**, there are significant and highly significant effects of Contamination and Site.
- These results support the conclusion that Contamination and nested Site structure influence H1 and H2, with strong evidence of group differences.


*SEE EXPLANATORY SLIDES*

## Checking differences in sequencing depth

Everything so far looks nice. BUT, are the counts across samples
similar?

```{r chunk_7d58a6}
View(rowSums(tDat))   # What is the sum of counts for each sample?
range(rowSums(tDat))  # There is as little as 336 counts for one sample
                      # and as much as 5911 for another! (one order of magnitude diff.)
```

Presence-absence metrics and several Alpha diversity metrics are
affected by differences in counts/reads across samples, thus we have to
account for that if we are interested in such metrics.

### Rarefaction curves

One way to do this is to rarefy to the sample with the lower number of
counts, which we can do using the Vegan package.

```{r chunk_9465b9}
(raremax <- min(rowSums(tDat))) #To rarefy to the sample with the lower number of counts
Sr <- rarefy(tDat, raremax)
plot(S, Sr, xlab = "Observed No. of taxa", ylab = "Rarefied No. of taxa")
abline(0, 1)
rarecurve(tDat, step = 20, sample = raremax, col = "blue", cex = 0.6)

View(Sr)
range(Sr)
```

We see that one sample 'ALB6' has clearly more reads than the others.
This happens sometimes when samples were processed in different
sequencing runs, which can introduce procedural bias to our analysis.

Note that a lot of OTUs are trimmed in this case .Rarefaction is not
good for this data set if you are interested in the rare OTUs / taxons
(or ASVs, depending on your dataset).

This procedure does work for other types of datasets, depending on your
question. See some examples of our papers using ASV and Metagenomics
data below: <https://www.nature.com/articles/s41522-022-00301-3>
<https://www.nature.com/articles/s41522-019-0079-4>
<https://journals.asm.org/doi/10.1128/msystems.00471-20?permanently=true#figS6>
<https://pubs.acs.org/doi/10.1021/acsestwater.1c00018>

### Alpha-diversity metrics on rarefied data

Now we will re-calculate alpha-diversity metrics on the OTU matrix
rarefied to the lowest number of counts.

```{r chunk_13dcc5}
Dat_r<-apply(tDat, 1, function(x)round(x/sum(x)*min(rowSums(tDat))))

# The 'apply' function accepts vectors and matrices as input and can return vectors or matrices work in the rows. Here there is an 'apply' function with tDat matrix as input, 1 means apply the function on the rows, and the third argument is the function that we want to apply on each element of this matrix.

# lets see if data were imported correctly
head(Dat_r)

# Lets see what are the variables in these data 
str(Dat_r)  # display the internal structure of the object 'Dat_r'
str(Fac1) 

# how many samples and variables does my data matrix have?
dim(Dat_r)
# Same: 45 variables (OTUs), 24 samples (2 contamination levels x 4 sites ea. x 3 replicates = 24)

tDat_r = t(Dat_r) # transpose the data as vegan wants variables (taxa, OTUs, ASVs, etc.) as columns
```

Make a new dataframe with Hill numbers based on rarefied data:

```{r chunk_5bd853}
S_r <- specnumber(tDat_r) # observed number of species (Richness)
range(S_r) # We observe that richness varies now from 12 to 23 taxa, accounting for differences in sequencing depth

# Hill alpha diversity indices  (Hill, 1973)
H0_r <- S_r #Renaming richness to Hill #0
H1_r <- exp(diversity(tDat_r, index = "shannon"))
H2_r <- diversity(tDat_r, index = "invsimpson")

# Make a dataframe with Hill numbers (including metadata)
Hill_r <- data.frame(Fac1, H0_r, H1_r, H2_r)

## Change order of factor levels for plots so that "Low" comes first:
Hill_r$Contamination <- factor(Hill_r$Contamination, levels = c("Low", "High"))

# View new dataframe
view(Hill_r)
```

### Visualisation - Box plots

#### Plot Hill numbers on rarefied data using box plots:

```{r chunk_bf9e98}
box_H0_r <- ggplot(data = Hill_r, mapping = aes(x = Contamination, y = H0_r))+
  geom_boxplot(notch = FALSE, fill = "lightgray", width = 0.3, outlier.size = 2)+
  stat_summary(fun = mean, geom = "point", #This is to add the mean values to our plot
               shape = 18, size = 4, color = "darkred", alpha = 0.6)+
  geom_jitter(width = 0.15, alpha = 0.4)+ #To draw the data points too      
  theme_linedraw()+
  theme(panel.grid.major = element_blank(), panel.grid.minor = element_blank())+
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 0))+
  theme(text = element_text(size=15))

box_H1_r <- ggplot(data = Hill_r, mapping = aes(x = Contamination, y = H1_r))+
  geom_boxplot(notch = FALSE, fill = "lightgray", width = 0.3, outlier.size = 2)+
  stat_summary(fun = mean, geom = "point", #This is to add the mean values to our plot
               shape = 18, size = 4, color = "darkred", alpha = 0.6)+
  geom_jitter(width = 0.15, alpha = 0.4)+ #To draw the data points too      
  theme_linedraw()+
  theme(panel.grid.major = element_blank(), panel.grid.minor = element_blank())+
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 0))+
  theme(text = element_text(size=15))

box_H2_r <- ggplot(data = Hill_r, mapping = aes(x = Contamination, y = H2_r))+
  geom_boxplot(notch = FALSE, fill = "lightgray", width = 0.3, outlier.size = 2)+
  stat_summary(fun = mean, geom = "point", #This is to add the mean values to our plot
               shape = 18, size = 4, color = "darkred", alpha = 0.6)+
  geom_jitter(width = 0.15, alpha = 0.4)+ #To draw the data points too      
  theme_linedraw()+
  theme(panel.grid.major = element_blank(), panel.grid.minor = element_blank())+
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 0))+
  theme(text = element_text(size=15))

ggarrange(box_H0_r, box_H1_r, box_H2_r, ncol = 3, nrow = 1) ### See plots
```

#### Unrarefied vs. rarefied alpha-diversity plots:

```{r chunk_5ce50c}
Plot <- ggarrange(box_H0,  box_H0_r, 
          box_H1,  box_H1_r,
          box_H2,  box_H2_r,  ncol = 2, nrow = 3) ### See plots side by side
```

We can compare these plots better if y-axes limits are the same, we can
do this by adjusting their y-axes:

```{r chunk_45b11f}
box_H0 <- box_H0 + ylim(10,25)
box_H0_r <- box_H0_r + ylim(10,25)
box_H1 <- box_H1 + ylim(2,10)
box_H1_r <- box_H1_r + ylim(2,10)
box_H2 <- box_H2 + ylim(2,8)
box_H2_r <- box_H2_r + ylim(2,8)

#Let's look again at unrarefied vs. rarefied data
ggarrange(box_H0,  box_H0_r, 
          box_H1,  box_H1_r,
          box_H2,  box_H2_r,  ncol = 2, nrow = 3) ### See plots side by side
```

You can also look at these data with raincloud plots, as we did above
for unrarefied data.

*SEE EXPLANATORY SLIDES*

## Distance-based multivariate analyses to compare community structure

**NOTE:** Some of the steps below may have happened already during the bioinformatics steps (e.g., normalization during pre-processing).

---

### Data Standardization

We would like to account for sampling depth by using the relative abundance of the sequence counts. We will do this manually using a few steps:

- We need to transpose the data if needed (samples should be in rows)  
- Divide each sample by its total  
- Multiply by 100 — this results in relative abundance (%)  

**Note:** If you have already accounted for differences in sample depth in your pipeline (e.g., normalization, rarefaction, etc.), you don't need to do this step here.

```{r chunk_rel_abund}
## Calculate relative abundance (%)
tDat1 <- tDat / rowSums(tDat) * 100  

## View the resulting table to confirm the transformation
view(tDat1)
```

---

### Data Transformation

We will calculate distances/dissimilarities between each pair of samples using the Bray-Curtis measure.

To avoid focusing only on the few abundant taxa, it is a good idea to transform the data to give more weight to less abundant taxa and thus look at the "whole" community rather than just ~10 abundant taxa.

We will use here a **square root transformation**, which reduces the influence of dominant taxa.

```{r chunk_sqrt}
## Apply square-root transformation to down-weight dominant taxa
Dat1.sqrt <- sqrt(tDat1)

## View transformed data
view(Dat1.sqrt)
```

*SEE EXPLANATORY SLIDES*

---

### Calculate Bray-Curtis dissimilarity matrix

```{r chunk_bc_dist}
## Calculate Bray-Curtis dissimilarity matrix
Dat1.bc <- vegdist(Dat1.sqrt, method = "bray")  

## Many other distance measures exist — check ?vegdist for options
## Example: Jaccard is useful for presence/absence analyses (composition only)

## View the dissimilarity matrix (lower triangle format, hard to view!)
Dat1.bc  

## Optional: Convert to full symmetric matrix to view all pairwise distances
# as.matrix(Dat1.bc)
```

The distances represent dissimilarity between samples, where:

- 0 = exactly the same  
- 1 = completely different  

Note that some software can represent these values as similarity (1 - dissimilarity), so always pay attention to how distances are reported!

*SEE EXPLANATORY SLIDES*

---

### Data Visualisation

Remember to always **VISUALIZE the data BEFORE running any stats.** Ordination helps explore patterns in community structure.

---

### Plot an nMDS ordination

Non-metric multidimensional scaling (nMDS) is an ordination method. This is a 2D map of your samples, where:

- Closer points = more similar communities  
- Distant points = more different communities  

**How well is the multidimensional data represented in two dimensions?**  
We look at two key diagnostics:  

1. **Stress value** — A low stress value (commonly < 0.2) indicates a better fit  
2. **Stressplot** — Diagnostic plot showing how well distances are preserved  
   - Points close to the line = good representation of the true distances  

```{r chunk_NMDS}
## Perform the NMDS analysis
tDat1.mds <- metaMDS(Dat1.bc, autotransform = FALSE, trace = FALSE, trymax = 50)

## Print NMDS results including stress value
print(tDat1.mds)

## Plot the NMDS ordination
plot(tDat1.mds)

## Add a stressplot to assess ordination quality
stressplot(tDat1.mds)
```

---

#### Choosing Distance Measures & NMDS Cautions

**Q: Why do we choose Bray-Curtis or Euclidean for beta diversity analysis?**

- **Bray-Curtis** is preferred for abundance data (like microbial communities):
  - Accounts for both presence/absence and abundance
  - Ignores double-zeroes (shared absences), which is common in sparse datasets  
- **Euclidean distance** measures straight-line distances:
  - Suitable for continuous, normally distributed data
  - **NOT recommended** for community composition/count data, as it doesn't handle many zeros well and is sensitive to large values  

In microbial ecology, **Bray-Curtis** is the standard choice for beta diversity with abundance data.

---

**Q: Are there conditions where NMDS is not appropriate?**

- NMDS is flexible (non-parametric) and does **not assume** Euclidean space, making it suitable for ecological data.  
- But be cautious:
  - If your distance matrix has many tied ranks or poor structure, NMDS may fail to converge  
  - If the **stress value is too high** (commonly > 0.2), the 2D map is unreliable  
- In NMDS:
  - Axes do **not** explain variance in the traditional sense  
  - We rely on stress value and stressplot to assess the quality of the ordination  

*SEE SLIDES OR NOTES FOR FURTHER EXAMPLES*


#### Let's improve the nMDS plot's aesthetics by adding colours and symbols to make the groupings clearer

```{r chunk_NMDS_add_colours}
# Plot with dots coloured by contamination level (e.g., High vs Low)
plot(tDat1.mds$points, col = Fac1$Contamination, pch = 16)

# Add a legend to indicate colours
legend("topright", legend = c("High", "Low"), pch = 16, col = c("black", "red"))
```

---

```{r chunk_NMDS_add_symbols}
# Add both colours for contamination and different symbols for sites
plot(tDat1.mds, type = "n")  # Empty ordination space

# Points coloured by contamination and shaped by site
points(tDat1.mds, display = "sites", 
       pch = c(16, 8, 17, 11, 15, 9, 18, 12)[as.numeric(Fac1$Site)], 
       col = c("black", "red")[as.numeric(Fac1$Contamination)])

# Legend for sites and contamination levels
legend("topright", legend = c(levels(Fac1$Site), levels(Fac1$Contamination)), 
       pch = c(16, 8, 17, 11, 15, 9, 18, 12, 16, 16), 
       col = c(rep("black", length(levels(Fac1$Site))), "black", "red"), 
       bty = "n", cex = 1)

# Add stress value to the plot
legend("bottomright", legend = paste("Stress =", round(tDat1.mds$stress, 2)), bty = "n")
```

---

```{r chunk_NMDS_add_sample_names}
# Alternatively, label points by sample name instead of using symbols
plot(tDat1.mds, type = "n")  # Empty ordination space

# Add sample names, coloured by contamination level
orditorp(tDat1.mds, display = "sites", cex = 0.6, air = 0.01, 
         col = c("black", "red")[as.numeric(Fac1$Contamination)])

# Add legend for contamination level
legend("topright", legend = levels(Fac1$Contamination), 
       pch = 16, col = c("black", "red"), bty = "n", cex = 1)

# Add stress value to the plot
legend("bottomright", legend = paste("Stress =", round(tDat1.mds$stress, 2)), bty = "n")
```

---

### Plot a dendrogram (Cluster diagram)

```{r chunk_cluster_diagram}
# Hierarchical clustering of the Bray-Curtis distances using average linkage
Dat1.hclus <- hclust(Dat1.bc, method = "average")

# Plot the resulting dendrogram
plot(Dat1.hclus, hang = -1, main = "Cluster Diagram (Bray-Curtis)", 
     xlab = "Samples", ylab = "Dissimilarity")
```


We can see that some samples (ABi5, ABi6, ABi7) are approximately 0.3 dissimilar (or share 0.7 similarity), on average, with the rest of the samples. 
Or that ABi5 and ABi6 are approximately 0.13 dissimilar (or share 0.87 similarity).

Here's another way to visualise the clusters using rectangles on the dendrogram:

```{r chunk_cluster_diagram_v2}
cl <- Dat1.hclus
plot(cl, hang = -1, main = "Cluster Diagram with Cluster Boundaries")
# Add rectangles to highlight clusters at different dissimilarity levels
rect.hclust(cl, h = 0.22, border = "grey")
rect.hclust(cl, h = 0.15, border = "blue")
```

### Add cluster-enclosing ellipses to the NMDS plot

We can overlay ellipses on the NMDS plot based on the clusters identified from hierarchical clustering.

```{r chunk_NMDS_add_cluster_ellipses}
# NMDS plot with colours for contamination levels
plot(tDat1.mds$points, col = Fac1$Contamination, pch = 16)
legend("topright", legend = c("High", "Low"), pch = 16, col = c("black", "red"))

# Add ellipses around groups identified by hierarchical clustering
ordiellipse(tDat1.mds, cutree(cl, h = 0.22), kind = "ehull", col = "grey")
ordiellipse(tDat1.mds, cutree(cl, h = 0.15), kind = "ehull", col = "blue")
```

Same idea, but using different symbols for the different sites:

```{r chunk_NMDS_add_cluster_ellipses_site_symbols}
# Empty NMDS plot
plot(tDat1.mds, type = "n")

# Add points with symbols representing sites and colours representing contamination levels
points(tDat1.mds, display = "sites",
       pch = c(16, 8, 17, 11, 15, 9, 18, 12)[as.numeric(Fac1$Site)],
       col = c("black", "red")[as.numeric(Fac1$Contamination)])

# Add legends for sites and contamination levels
legend("topright", inset = c(-0.35, 0), xpd = TRUE, horiz = FALSE,
       legend = c(levels(Fac1$Site), levels(Fac1$Contamination)),
       pch = c(16, 8, 17, 11, 15, 9, 18, 12, 16, 16),
       col = c(rep("black", length(levels(Fac1$Site))), "black", "red"),
       bty = "n", cex = 1)

# Add stress value
legend("bottomright", legend = paste("Stress =", round(tDat1.mds$stress, 2)), bty = "n")

# Add ellipses based on clusters
ordiellipse(tDat1.mds, cutree(cl, h = 0.22), kind = "ehull", col = "grey")
```

There is plenty of material online on how to make NMDS plots. For example: https://jonlefcheck.net/2012/10/24/nmds-tutorial-in-r/

### Plot a PCoA (Principal Coordinates Analysis) for the same dataset

PCoA is another ordination method, often used in exploratory data analysis to visualise patterns of similarity or dissimilarity among samples. 
Unlike NMDS, PCoA is based on preserving the original distances as faithfully as possible along orthogonal axes.

```{r chunk_PCoA_plot}
# Calculate Principal Coordinates Analysis (PCoA)
Dat1.pcoa <- cmdscale(Dat1.bc, eig = TRUE, k = 2)  # k = 2 for 2D plot

# Plot PCoA with colours for contamination level
plot(Dat1.pcoa$points, col = Fac1$Contamination, pch = 16, 
     xlab = paste0("PCoA1 (", round(Dat1.pcoa$eig[1] / sum(Dat1.pcoa$eig), 2) * 100, "%)"),
     ylab = paste0("PCoA2 (", round(Dat1.pcoa$eig[2] / sum(Dat1.pcoa$eig), 2) * 100, "%)"),
     main = "PCoA Plot (Bray-Curtis)")

# Add legend for contamination level
legend("topright", legend = c("High", "Low"), pch = 16, col = c("black", "red"))
```

### Add symbols to the PCoA
```{r chunk_PCoA_plot_symbols}
# Expand right margin before plotting
par(mar = c(5, 4, 4, 8))  

# Calculate Principal Coordinates Analysis (PCoA)
Dat1.pcoa <- cmdscale(Dat1.bc, eig = TRUE, k = 2)  # k = 2 for 2D plot

# Empty plot space
plot(Dat1.pcoa$points, type = "n",
     xlab = paste0("PCoA1 (", round(Dat1.pcoa$eig[1] / sum(Dat1.pcoa$eig), 2) * 100, "%)"),
     ylab = paste0("PCoA2 (", round(Dat1.pcoa$eig[2] / sum(Dat1.pcoa$eig), 2) * 100, "%)"),
     main = "PCoA Plot (Bray-Curtis) with Symbols for Sites")

# Points shaped by site and coloured by contamination
points(Dat1.pcoa$points,
       pch = c(16, 8, 17, 11, 15, 9, 18, 12)[as.numeric(Fac1$Site)],
       col = c("black", "red")[as.numeric(Fac1$Contamination)])

# Legend for site symbols and contamination colours
usr <- par("usr")
legend(usr[2] + 0.02, usr[4], xpd = TRUE,
       legend = c(levels(Fac1$Site), levels(Fac1$Contamination)),
       pch = c(16, 8, 17, 11, 15, 9, 18, 12, 16, 16),
       col = c(rep("black", length(levels(Fac1$Site))), "black", "red"),
       bty = "n", cex = 1)

```

### Plot NMDS and PCoA side by side for comparison
```{r chunk_NMDS_PCoA_side_by_side, fig.width=12, fig.height=6}
par(mfrow = c(1, 2))

# NMDS plot with flipped x-axis
par(mar = c(5, 4, 4, 8))  # expand right margin
plot(-scores(tDat1.mds, display = "sites")[,1], 
      scores(tDat1.mds, display = "sites")[,2], type = "n",
     xlab = "NMDS1 (flipped)",
     ylab = "NMDS2",
     xaxt = "n", yaxt = "n",
     main = "NMDS with Symbols for Sites (x-axis flipped)")
points(-scores(tDat1.mds, display = "sites")[,1],
        scores(tDat1.mds, display = "sites")[,2],
       pch = c(16, 8, 17, 11, 15, 9, 18, 12)[as.numeric(Fac1$Site)],
       col = c("black", "red")[as.numeric(Fac1$Contamination)])
usr <- par("usr")
legend(usr[2] + 0.02, usr[4], xpd = TRUE,
       legend = c(levels(Fac1$Site), levels(Fac1$Contamination)),
       pch = c(16, 8, 17, 11, 15, 9, 18, 12, 16, 16),
       col = c(rep("black", length(levels(Fac1$Site))), "black", "red"),
       bty = "n", cex = 1)
legend("bottomleft", legend = paste("Stress =", round(tDat1.mds$stress, 2)), bty = "n")

# PCoA plot
par(mar = c(5, 4, 4, 8))  # expand right margin
plot(Dat1.pcoa$points, type = "n",
     xlab = paste0("PCoA1 (", round(Dat1.pcoa$eig[1] / sum(Dat1.pcoa$eig), 2) * 100, "%)"),
     ylab = paste0("PCoA2 (", round(Dat1.pcoa$eig[2] / sum(Dat1.pcoa$eig), 2) * 100, "%)"),
     main = "PCoA with Symbols for Sites")
points(Dat1.pcoa$points,
       pch = c(16, 8, 17, 11, 15, 9, 18, 12)[as.numeric(Fac1$Site)],
       col = c("black", "red")[as.numeric(Fac1$Contamination)])
usr <- par("usr")
legend(usr[2] + 0.02, usr[4], xpd = TRUE,
       legend = c(levels(Fac1$Site), levels(Fac1$Contamination)),
       pch = c(16, 8, 17, 11, 15, 9, 18, 12, 16, 16),
       col = c(rep("black", length(levels(Fac1$Site))), "black", "red"),
       bty = "n", cex = 1)
```

---

### Notes on Differences Between PCoA and NMDS

- **PCoA** (Principal Coordinates Analysis) is a metric, linear ordination method:
  - It attempts to preserve original distances (such as Bray-Curtis) as much as possible in a reduced number of axes.
  - Axes are orthogonal and ordered by decreasing importance (first axis explains the most variation).
  - The proportion of variance explained by each axis is meaningful and can be interpreted (e.g., Axis 1 explains X% of the variation).
  - The **sum of R² of all axes never exceeds 1**, because R² represents the proportion of variation explained.
  
- **NMDS** is a non-metric, rank-based ordination method:
  - It represents the ranks of pairwise distances rather than preserving absolute distances.
  - Axes do not have inherent meaning or order; interpretation focuses on the **stress value**.
  - The lower the stress value, the better the 2D ordination represents the multidimensional distance structure.
  - Axes cannot be interpreted in terms of explained variance like in PCoA.
  - NMDS is more robust to outliers and avoids artefacts like the **horseshoe effect**, which can occur with PCoA if community differences between samples are very large.

---

### Why not PCA?

- **PCA** (Principal Components Analysis) is a linear ordination method that assumes **Euclidean distances** between samples:
  - It is not recommended for community composition data like microbial datasets, which are typically high-dimensional, sparse, and non-linear.
  - Using PCA on Bray-Curtis or ecological distance matrices is incorrect and may lead to misleading results.
  - PCA should not be used for microbial datasets with non-Euclidean distances, as it violates core assumptions of the method.

*SEE EXPLANATORY SLIDES*


### HYPOTHESIS TESTING: Are the differences between groups statistically significant?

#### Statistical analysis - PERMANOVA (see papers by M.J. Anderson)

We use PERMANOVA (Permutational Multivariate Analysis of Variance) to test whether community composition differs between groups, in this case based on **Contamination**, while accounting for **Site nested within Contamination**.

```{r chunk_PERMANOVA_nested}
# Run PERMANOVA with nested structure (Site nested within Contamination)
adonis2(Dat1.bc ~ Contamination / Site, data = Fac1)
```

**Explanation of the code:**

- The **'/'** indicates that **Site** is nested within **Contamination**, meaning Sites are grouped within each contamination level, not crossed.
- PERMANOVA partitions the variance in community dissimilarity (Bray-Curtis) among these factors.
- Permutations are conducted appropriately given the nested structure.
- For recent R versions, see <https://search.r-project.org/CRAN/refmans/vegan/html/adonis.html> for updated permutation strategies.

**Interpretation of the output:**

- **Contamination**: Tests for differences in community composition between contamination levels, accounting for Sites within those levels. A significant p-value here indicates a true effect of Contamination.
- **Contamination:Site**: This term represents the variability among Sites within each Contamination level. It is not an interaction term but reflects natural variation at the Site level.
- **Residual**: Remaining unexplained variation.

In this example, both **Contamination** and **Site nested within Contamination** are statistically significant (**p = 0.001**), suggesting strong evidence for differences in community composition both across Contamination levels and among Sites.

*SEE EXPLANATORY SLIDES*

---

#### TESTS FOR MULTIVARIATE DISPERSION (PERMDISP)

Visual inspection of the nMDS plot suggested that bacterial communities at **high contamination** sites may exhibit greater dispersion (i.e., more variability) than those at **low contamination** sites.

We test this formally using PERMDISP, which assesses differences in multivariate dispersion (similar to Levene's test for homogeneity of variance). It is good practice to run PERMDISP alongside PERMANOVA to distinguish whether group differences are due to location (centroid differences) or dispersion.

```{r chunk_PERMDISP_test}
# Calculate distances to group centroids (multivariate spread)
Dat1.disp = betadisper(Dat1.bc, Fac1$Contamination, type = "centroid")

# Visualise with a boxplot
par(pty = 's')
boxplot(Dat1.disp, main = "Distance to Centroid by Contamination")

# Statistical test for differences in dispersion
permutest(Dat1.disp)
```

**Interpretation of the output:**

- The boxplot shows the distance of each sample to its group centroid (mean position) for each contamination level.
- The PERMDISP test p-value is **0.083**, which is **not statistically significant**, suggesting that the groups do not differ significantly in their dispersion.
- This supports the interpretation that the significant differences detected by PERMANOVA reflect genuine shifts in community composition (location effects), rather than differences in variability (dispersion) among groups.

**Conclusion:**

- There are significant differences in community composition between contamination levels, even after accounting for Site-level variation (nested design).
- There is no evidence that differences in group dispersion drive these results, strengthening confidence that the PERMANOVA results reflect true ecological differences.



## Which taxa are contributing most to the differences between groups? / Which taxa differ significantly between groups?

*SEE EXPLANATORY SLIDES*

Here, we will use the raw data (not dissimilarities) and run univariate
tests for each variable using the 'mvabund' package.

This is a way better approach as to using something like 'SIMPER'
('similarity percentages', Clarke 1993, Australian Journal of Ecology),
which is not a statistical test.

mvabund runs Generalised Linear Models to each response variable and you
can specify an appropriate distribution that, for instance, accounts for
mean-variance relationships appropriately. It also provides a
multivariate test statistic that accounts for correlations among
variables (so in essence is an alternative statistical multivariate
approach to distance-based analyses such as PERMANOVA; see Wang et al.
2012, Methods in Ecol. & Evol.)

install.packages('mvabund') \# Run once only to download to the computer
library('mvabund') \# Load the package

```{r chunk_65aa47}
dat.mv = mvabund(tDat) # makes an mvabund object with the bacterial data (the transposed matrix of RAW COUNTS)
```

We will use RAW COUNTS (hence 'tDat'), but we still need to account for
differences in sequencing depth. We will follow the approach proposed by
McMurdie & Holmes 2014 (PLOS Comput. Biol.), which is to include the
total number of counts per sample explicitly in the model.

```{r chunk_f4550f}
# Lets add a column to Fac1 (the spreadsheet with covariates) of sums of counts per sample 
Fac1$Counts = rowSums(tDat)
head(Fac1)

range(Fac1$Counts) #range of count values
```

We can have a quick look at the spread of our data using the boxplot
function

```{r chunk_283a70}
# Set margins for the plot (bottom, left, top, right)
par(mar = c(4, 20, 2, 2))

# Adjust text size for axis labels
par(cex.axis = 0.4)  # Adjust the value as needed (e.g., 0.8 for 80% of default size)

# Create a horizontal boxplot of tDat
boxplot(tDat, horizontal = TRUE, las = 2, main = "Abundance")

```

It looks like some taxa (e.g. Bacteria Gammaproteobacteria) are much
more abundant and variable than others (e.g. Cymadusa munnu). It's
probably a good idea to check our mean-variance relationship then!

We can do this using the meanvar.plot function:

```{r chunk_meanvar.plot}
# Set the plot window size before calling meanvar.plot()
dev.new(width = 8, height = 8)  # Adjust width and height as needed

# Run meanvar.plot and modify axis labels after
meanvar.plot(dat.mv)
title(xlab = "Taxa mean", ylab = "Taxa variance")

```

You can clearly see that the species with high means (on the x axis)
also have high variances (y axis).

Now let's get back to our research questions. - Which taxa are
contributing most to the differences between groups? - Which taxa differ
significantly between groups?

Let's start by eyeballing the data:

We will use ggplot2 to contrast abundances against 'Contamination':

```{r chunk_abundance_boxplot_by_contamination, fig.height=12, fig.width=8}
library(ggplot2)
library(tidyr)
library(dplyr)

# Reshape your data
dat_long <- dat.mv %>%
  as.data.frame() %>%
  mutate(Contamination = Fac1$Contamination) %>%
  pivot_longer(cols = -Contamination, names_to = "Taxon", values_to = "Abundance")

# Log-transform abundance, adding a small constant to avoid log(0)
dat_long <- dat_long %>%
  mutate(Abundance_log = log(Abundance + 1))

# Create the plot
ggplot(dat_long, aes(x = Abundance_log, y = Taxon, color = Contamination)) +
  geom_boxplot(outlier.size = 1) +
  theme_linedraw() +
  theme(panel.grid.major = element_blank(),
        panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
        axis.text.y = element_text(size = 8)) +
  labs(title = "Abundances by Taxon and Contamination",
       x = expression(paste("Abundances [log(", y, " + 1) scale]")),
       y = "") +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("Low" = "black", "High" = "red"))
```

### Fit the model using a poisson distribution
Lets fit the model using a poisson distribution - fits count data (e.g. number
of sequences)

```{r chunk_poisson_dist}
dat.nb = manyglm(dat.mv ~ Counts + Contamination, family="poisson", data=Fac1)

```

Before we examine the output, we need to check our model assumptions. We
can use the plot function to generate a plot of residuals.

#### Check the model fitting by plotting the Residuals:

```{r chunk_res_vs_fitted}
plot(dat.nb,n.vars=15) # involves some randomization, repeat it a couple of times
```

Fan pattern among multiple plots - bad model fit. This could mean that
one of our assumptions was wrong: either our mean-variance relationship
was wrongly specified, or our assumed relationship between our response
and predictors was incorrect. Or, we could have missed a key explaining
variable in our model which leaves a lot of unexplained variance.

### Fit the model using a negative binomial distribution:
Lets now fit the model using a negative binomial dist. - fits count data
(e.g. number of sequences):

```{r chunk_manyglm}
dat.nb = manyglm(dat.mv ~ Counts + Contamination, family="negative.binomial", data=Fac1)

```

We can deal with variance by choosing a family of GLMs with an
appropriate mean-variance assumption.

The default family used by mvabund when fitting multivariate GLMs is
negative binomial which assumes a quadratic mean-variance relationship
and a log-linear relationship between the response variables and any
continuous variables.

#### Check the model fitting plotting the residuals:

```{r chunk_res_vs_fit_modelfit}
plot(dat.nb,n.vars=15) # involves some randomisation, repeat it a couple of times
# No obvious pattern among multiple plots - a good model fit.
```

### Run a statistical test on the Model:

```{r chunk_model_test}
dat.aov = anova(dat.nb, nBoot=999, p.uni="adjusted") # used nBoot = 100 for trial, 999 for publication
```

#### Get the output of the test:

```{r chunk_model_test_output}
dat.aov$uni.p # univariate adjusted p values, note we are interested in row 2
# the output is wordy
```

### Show only those OTUs that are significantly different

```{r chunk_model_test_sig_diff}
diffs=which(dat.aov$uni.p[3,]<0.05) # you could also ask for < 0.1 so we catch the group slightly above 0.05
diffs
```

#### Plot the abundances of only these significanly different groups:

```{r chunk_abundances_ggplot}
# Subset dat.mv to only the taxa/groups in 'diffs'
dat_sub <- dat.mv[, diffs]

# Reshape to long format
dat_long <- dat_sub %>%
  as.data.frame() %>%
  mutate(Contamination = Fac1$Contamination) %>%
  pivot_longer(cols = -Contamination, names_to = "Taxon", values_to = "Abundance")

# Create boxplot with taxa on y-axis
ggplot(dat_long, aes(x = Abundance, y = Taxon, color = Contamination)) +
  geom_boxplot(outlier.size = 1) +
  theme_linedraw() +
  theme(panel.grid.major = element_blank(),
        panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
        axis.text.y = element_text(size = 10)) +
  labs(title = "Abundances of Selected Groups by Contamination",
       x = "Count Abundance", y = "") +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("Low" = "black", "High" = "red"))
```


### Example Heatmap of Abundance Data by Contamination

Heatmaps are a useful visualisation to show patterns of abundance across samples or groups. Below, we generate a heatmap based on the same abundance data used in the boxplots, summarised by **Contamination** and **Taxon**.

```{r chunk_abundance_heatmap, fig.height=8, fig.width=6}
# Reshape and summarise the data
dat_heat <- dat.mv %>%
  as.data.frame() %>%
  mutate(Contamination = Fac1$Contamination) %>%
  pivot_longer(cols = -Contamination, names_to = "Taxon", values_to = "Abundance") %>%
  mutate(Contamination = factor(Contamination, levels = c("Low", "High"))) %>%
  group_by(Contamination, Taxon) %>%
  summarise(Mean_Abundance = mean(Abundance), .groups = "drop")

# Create the heatmap
ggplot(dat_heat, aes(x = Contamination, y = Taxon, fill = Mean_Abundance)) +
  geom_tile(color = "white") +
  scale_fill_gradient(low = "white", high = "red") +
  theme_linedraw() +
  theme(panel.grid.major = element_blank(),
        panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
        axis.text.y = element_text(size = 8),
        axis.text.x = element_text(size = 10, angle = 0, hjust = 0.5)) +
  labs(title = "Mean Abundances by Taxon and Contamination",
       x = "Contamination",
       y = "Taxon",
       fill = "Mean Abundance")
```

**Notes:**

- The heatmap displays the average abundance of each taxon for each Contamination level.
- Lighter colours indicate lower abundance, while darker colours (red) indicate higher abundance.
- You can modify the colour scale (e.g., using `scale_fill_viridis_c()` from the **viridis** package) for alternative colour schemes.
- This heatmap summarises patterns in abundance across groups and complements the boxplots showing individual data points.


#### Include Site information in the Heatmap of Abundance Data by Contamination

```{r chunk_abundance_heatmap_by_site, fig.height=8, fig.width=8}
# Reshape and summarise the data by Site and Contamination
dat_heat <- dat.mv %>%
  as.data.frame() %>%
  mutate(Contamination = Fac1$Contamination,
         Site = Fac1$Site) %>%
  pivot_longer(cols = -c(Contamination, Site), names_to = "Taxon", values_to = "Abundance") %>%
  group_by(Contamination, Site, Taxon) %>%
  summarise(Mean_Abundance = mean(Abundance), .groups = "drop") %>%
  mutate(Contamination = factor(Contamination, levels = c("Low", "High")),
         Site = as.factor(Site),
         Contam_Site = interaction(Contamination, Site, sep = "_"))

# Create the heatmap with one column per Site within each Contamination group
ggplot(dat_heat, aes(x = Contam_Site, y = Taxon, fill = Mean_Abundance)) +
  geom_tile(color = "white") +
  scale_fill_gradient(low = "white", high = "red") +
  theme_linedraw() +
  theme(panel.grid.major = element_blank(),
        panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
        axis.text.x = element_text(size = 8, angle = 45, hjust = 1),
        axis.text.y = element_text(size = 8)) +
  labs(title = "Mean Abundances by Taxon and Site within Contamination",
       x = "Site (grouped by Contamination)",
       y = "Taxon",
       fill = "Mean Abundance")
```

*SEE EXPLANATORY SLIDES*

## Which environmental variables relate to the bacterial multivariate structure?

A tool we will use for this is called distance-based Redundancy Analysis
(dbRDA) - McArdle & Anderson (Ecology 82, 290-297; 2001). This is a type
of constrained ordination.

We need to import the chemistry data, which is a subset of gas
chromatography/mass spectrometry data of chemical extracts from the
surface of the coralline algae samples, with the relative abundance of
these biomarkers given (i.e. chemical composition of the seaweed hosts).

Note that "X" has no meaning in the variable names of this example (just
some environmental data).

We want to test for relationships between these data and the bacterial
community structure

### Load the environmental data

```{r chunk_e0fa08}
# Import environmental variables directly from the public course repository
Env1 <- read.delim(
  "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/ezesantillan/SCELSE-summer-course-experimental-design-data-analysis/main/data/Environmental_data.txt",
  row.names = 1
)
```

### Data Pre-processing

Before we proceed with the analysis, we need to: A) Check for strong
correlations between covariates - if so, remove some from that set B)
Check for even spread - if not, transform those variables that are
skewed C) Normalise to bring the to the same scale

install.packages('car') library(car)

Create a data visualization function:
