```{r chunk_aa4935}

panel.cor <- function(x, y, digits = 2, prefix = "", cex.cor, ...)
{
  usr <- par("usr"); on.exit(par(usr))
  par(usr = c(0, 1, 0, 1))
  r <- abs(cor(x, y))
  txt <- format(c(r, 0.123456789), digits = digits)[1]
  txt <- paste0(prefix, txt)
  if(missing(cex.cor)) cex.cor <- 0.8/strwidth(txt)
  text(0.5, 0.5, txt, cex = cex.cor * r+0.75)
}
```

#### Visualize the data:

```{r scatterplot_env1}
scatterplotMatrix(Env1,
                  diagonal = "histogram",
                  upper.panel = panel.cor)  # Correlation in upper panel
```

Note that the X380 is showing some uneven distribution and is highly
bunched on the left (green histogram). To spread this data out, a
transformation is required. We can log transform this variable.

#### Are there any others that need transformation?

```{r chunk_003e7c}
Env2 = Env1
Env2$X380 = log(Env2$X380) # log transform x380
```

Log transform on X380 doesn't really remove the skewedness of the data
to make it normally distributed. Yet, its better than before. This is
real data, so good to point out that sometimes that's all you can do and
thus need to be careful with conclusions.

#### Are there any correlated variables? Maybe 441.9 and 479 are.

```{r chunk_6bc5ef}
Rm1 = which(colnames(Env2)%in%c("X441.9")) # column number of X441.9
Env2 = Env2[,-Rm1] # remove X441.9
```

#### Let's visualize again, after the pre-processing we just did:

```{r scatterplot_env2}
scatterplotMatrix(Env2,
                 diagonal ="histogram",
                 upper.panel = panel.cor)
                  
```

#### Standardize the environmental variables. 
Standardization centers data around the mean and scales to a standard deviation of 1

Equation: (x – mean)/standard deviation

```{r chunk_cd48d6}
Env2.norm = scale(Env2)
Env2.norm = data.frame(Env2.norm)

Env2.norm[1:10,1:5] # See the first 10 rows and first 5 columns
```

#### Visualize again, after standardization:

```{r scatterplot_env2norm}

scatterplotMatrix(Env2.norm,
                 diagonal ="histogram",
                  upper.panel = panel.cor # Correlation in upper panel
                  )

```

Now we are ready for the analysis.

For that we need our square-root transformed (transposed, standardized) OTU
table: Dat1.sqrt

### Distance-based redundancy analysis (dbRDA)

We will first define mod0, an object that stores the result of a dbRDA
where Dat1.sqrt is the response data, Env2.norm is the environmental
data, and the distance method used is "bray" (Bray-Curtis distance).

The formula Dat1.sqrt \~ 1 means that no environmental variables are
used in the model (a model with only the intercept), similar to
Non-metric Multidimensional Scaling (NMDS). This means that mod0 is
unconstrained. We will use this later in model selection.

```{r chunk_d09774}
mod0 = dbrda(Dat1.sqrt ~ 1, Env2.norm, distance = "bray") # the 1 means to no variables. Same as our NMDS above

```

Now we will constrain our dbRDA. Here mod1 is an object that stores the
result of a dbRDA where Dat1.sqrt is constrained by all the variables in
Env2.norm.

The formula Dat1.sqrt \~ . indicates that all variables in Env2.norm are
used to constrain the ordination.

```{r chunk_4033b6}
mod1 = dbrda(Dat1.sqrt ~ ., Env2.norm, distance = "bray") # the . means all variables in Env constrained by model fitted
```

#### Marginal tests (i.e. each covariate on its own, independently of others)

The line below performs an Analysis of Variance (ANOVA) on mod1 to test
the significance of each variable independently (marginal tests).

The by="mar" argument specifies that the test should be done marginally
(i.e., for each covariate on its own).

The permu=999 argument specifies that 999 permutations should be used to
assess significance.

```{r chunk_efd748}
anova(mod1, by="mar", permu=999)
```

#### Overall Structure of the Output

1)  Header Information: Permutation test for dbrda under reduced model:
    Indicates that the test is based on the dbRDA model. Marginal
    effects of terms: Shows that the effects of each term (environmental
    variable) are tested independently. Permutation: free: Indicates
    that the permutations were conducted freely, without constraints.
    Number of permutations: 999: Specifies that 999 permutations were
    used to assess significance.

2)  Model Information: Model: dbrda(formula = Dat1.sqrt \~ X380 + X389 +
    X401 + X405.2 + X420 + X479 + X499 + X537.9 + X571.9, data =
    Env2.norm, distance = "bray"): The model formula used in the dbRDA,
    where Dat1.sqrt is the response variable and the terms on the
    right-hand side are the environmental variables included in the
    model. The Bray-Curtis distance is used.

3)  Interpretation of the Results Table: The table provides the
    following columns for each environmental variable: Df: Degrees of
    freedom for each variable. SumOfSqs: Sum of squares, representing
    the amount of variation explained by the variable. F: F-statistic
    for the variable, indicating the ratio of the explained variance to
    the unexplained variance. Pr(\>F): P-value for the F-statistic,
    showing the significance of the variable.

Significant Variables: Only X380 is significant at the 0.05 level,
indicating it has a significant effect on the community composition.

Non-significant Variables: All other variables (X389, X401, X405.2,
X420, X479, X499, X537.9, and X571.9) do not show significant effects.

#### Plot the dbRDA

Calculate percentage variance explained by each axis:
```{r chunk_49283b}
summary_mod1 <- summary(mod1)
percent_variance1 <- summary_mod1$cont$importance[2,] * 100
```

Plot the dbRDA constrained ordination results stored in mod1, with percentage variance on axes:

```{r chunk_70e054}
plot(mod1, main = "dbRDA - mod1", xlab = paste0("dbRDA1 (", round(percent_variance1[1], 2), "%)"), ylab = paste0("dbRDA2 (", round(percent_variance1[2], 2), "%)"))
orditorp(mod1, display = "sites", cex = 0.55, air = 0.01, col = c("black", "red")[as.numeric(Fac1$Contamination)])
legend("topright", legend = c("High", "Low"), pch = 16, col = c("black", "red"))
```

### Perform stepwise selection based on p-values

We can do model selection - "best" model considering all covariates and their combinations. Based on p-values, step wise selection.

```{r chunk_89ff97}
pval = ordistep(mod0, scope = formula(mod1), direction="both")
pval$anova
```

#### 'Best' Model Interpretation:

-   ordistep Function: The ordistep function is used to perform stepwise
    selection to determine the "best" dbRDA model by iteratively adding
    and/or removing environmental variables based on their p-values. It
    starts with the intercept-only model (mod0) and explores models up
    to the full model (mod1).
-   Stepwise Selection: The process considers both forward and backward
    directions, meaning it can add variables that improve the model and
    remove variables that do not significantly contribute to the model.
-   ANOVA Table: The output pval\$anova will show the results of the
    stepwise selection process, including the sequence of adding or
    removing variables, their corresponding F-values, and p-values. This
    table helps identify which variables were included in the final
    "best" model and their significance levels.


#### Plot the dbRDA with the 'Best' model

Calculate percentage variance explained by each axis for pval:
```{r chunk_2a2557}
summary_pval <- summary(pval)
percent_variance_pval <- summary_pval$cont$importance[2,] * 100
```

Plot the dBRDA:
```{r chunk_a7e724}
plot(pval, main = "dbRDA - pval", xlab = paste0("dbRDA1 (", round(percent_variance_pval[1], 2), "%)"), ylab = paste0("dbRDA2 (", round(percent_variance_pval[2], 2), "%)"))
orditorp(pval, display = "sites", cex = 0.55, air = 0.01, col = c("black", "red")[as.numeric(Fac1$Contamination)])
legend("topright", legend = c("High", "Low"), pch = 16, col = c("black", "red"))
```

#### Now try another model, based on Akaike's Information Criteria (AIC) for selection:

```{r chunk_2537fa}
AICselec = step(mod0, scope = formula(mod1), direction="both")
AICselec$anova
```

#### Plot the dbRDA for the AIC model:

Calculate percentage variance explained by each axis for AICselec:
```{r chunk_3f7a26}
summary_AICselec <- summary(AICselec)
percent_variance_AICselec <- summary_AICselec$cont$importance[2,] * 100
```

Plot AICselec with percentage variance on axes:
```{r chunk_213c16}
plot(AICselec, main = "dbRDA - AICselec", xlab = paste0("dbRDA1 (", round(percent_variance_AICselec[1], 2), "%)"), ylab = paste0("dbRDA2 (", round(percent_variance_AICselec[2], 2), "%)"))
orditorp(AICselec, display = "sites", cex = 0.55, air = 0.01, col = c("black", "red")[as.numeric(Fac1$Contamination)])
legend("topright", legend = c("High", "Low"), pch = 16, col = c("black", "red"))
```

Comparing our selection criteria: the "best" model above and the
Akaike's Information Criteria (AIC), penalty for additional parameters
is less using the AIC. Thus, when AIC will present the danger that it
would outfit, while the other will present the danger that it would
underfit.


# About the instructor

**Dr. Ezequiel Santillan**
Senior Research Fellow
Singapore Centre for Environmental Life Sciences Engineering (SCELSE)
Nanyang Technological University (NTU), Singapore

Research interests include microbial ecology, environmental biotechnology, resource recovery, and circular bioeconomy systems.

For questions, feedback, or suggestions regarding these course materials, please contact:

**Ezequiel Santillan**
Email: [esantillan@ntu.edu.sg](mailto:esantillan@ntu.edu.sg)
LinkedIn: https://www.linkedin.com/in/ezequielsantillan/

---

# Citation and reuse

These materials were originally developed for the **SCELSE Summer Course 2026: Experimental Design and Data Analysis**.

If you use or adapt these materials for teaching, workshops, or training purposes, please cite the repository or acknowledge the original source.

Suggested citation:

Santillan E. (2026). *SCELSE Summer Course 2026: Experimental Design and Data Analysis* (Version 1.0) [Course materials]. Zenodo. https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.20922744

Repository:
https://github.com/ezesantillan/SCELSE-summer-course-experimental-design-data-analysis

Course website:
https://ezesantillan.github.io/SCELSE-summer-course-experimental-design-data-analysis/
